findings.description: login() builds the SELECT with an f-string interpolating the caller-supplied email, allowing classic SQL injection (e.g. ' OR '1'='1 bypasses the email/is_active filter) instead of using a parameterized query.

validate_session has the expiry comparison inverted (expires_at > now treated as expired): every still-valid session is deleted and rejected on first validation while genuinely expired sessions fall through and return user_id, accepting expired tokens.

rotate_token inherits the inverted check in validate_session, so only already-expired sessions can be rotated and each rotation mints a fresh 12h token, letting expired sessions be extended indefinitely while valid sessions can never renew.

Lockout is permanent: LOCKOUT_MINUTES (line 17) is never used and failed_attempts only resets on successful login, which is unreachable once attempts >= MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS, so 5 bad guesses (attacker-triggerable) lock any account forever.

find_user_by_email loads the entire users table with SELECT * and fetchall, materializes every row into User objects, then linearly scans in Python instead of a parameterized WHERE email = ? query, giving O(N) time and memory per lookup.
PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 with 100,000 iterations is well below current OWASP guidance (~600,000 for SHA-256), weakening offline brute-force resistance of stored hashes.

Unknown emails return immediately while known emails incur a 100k-iteration PBKDF2 computation, creating a timing side channel that enables user enumeration; no dummy hash is computed for the unknown-email path.

Sessions live only in an in-process dict: they vanish on restart, are not shared across workers/instances, and grow without bound unless cleanup_expired is explicitly called by some external scheduler (none exists here).
validate_session performs an unsynchronized get-then-del on the shared _sessions dict, so two concurrent validations (or a concurrent logout) of the same token can race and raise KeyError on the del.

The lockout check uses the failed_attempts value read at SELECT time, so concurrent login attempts can all pass the threshold check before any increment commits, permitting somewhat more than MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS guesses (TOCTOU).

validate_session never re-checks the user against the database, so a deactivated (is_active=0) or deleted user retains a working session for up to the 12-hour TTL with no server-side revocation by user id.
find_user_by_email returns the full User record including password_hash and salt to any caller, unnecessarily propagating credential material beyond the auth boundary.

Email matching is inconsistent and unnormalized: find_user_by_email uses case-sensitive Python ==, while login relies on SQLite's collation, so User@x.com and user@x.com may resolve differently between the two paths and neither lowercases/trims input.
Raw session tokens are stored as plaintext dict keys server-side; hashing tokens at rest would prevent a memory dump or debug endpoint from yielding directly usable credentials.
datetime.utcnow() (also lines 105 and 147) is deprecated since Python 3.12 and produces naive datetimes; prefer datetime.now(timezone.utc) for timezone-aware timestamps.
